content.text: A property decorator receives the class prototype (or the constructor for static properties) and the property key. It cannot read the initial value because class fields are not on the prototype at decoration time — they are assigned per-instance in the constructor. Property decorators are typically used to
are property decorators provided by Angular. They register the decorated property with Angular's component metadata system so the template compiler knows which bindings to wire up.
A method decorator receives three arguments: the class prototype, the method name, and the
property holds the original function. You can
it to add before/after logic, or
it entirely by returning a new descriptor.
is Angular's built-in method decorator. It registers the decorated method as an event listener on the host element (or the global
). The event name and optional arguments list are the decorator's parameters.
A decorator can also target a getter or setter pair. It receives the same three arguments as a method decorator but the descriptor's
properties hold the accessor functions instead of
Combining Property and Method Decorators — Validation Example
A realistic example: property decorators record validation rules; a method decorator runs them before the method executes.
— a fourth kind that decorates individual parameters of a constructor or method. Angular's
token is a parameter decorator used to specify non-class DI tokens.
Testing Code Decorated with Property or Method Decorators
Execution Order — All Decorator Types Together
When a class has multiple decorator types, they are evaluated in this order:

content: Parameter decorators on the constructor and methods
Method, accessor, and property decorators — evaluated bottom-up on each method
Class decorators — evaluated bottom-up on the class
Property decorators receive (target, key) — no descriptor. They record metadata about fields, not modify values.
Method decorators receive (target, key, descriptor) — modify descriptor.value to wrap or replace the method.
Returning a new (or modified) descriptor from a method decorator replaces the original.
